narrative:
The Karnataka High Court heard **214 of 474 scheduled matters**, a **45.1% hearing rate**. That is marginally above the **44.3%** seen on **2026-04-30**, and clearly stronger than **38.0%** on **2026-04-17** and **31.5%** on **2026-04-24**. The day was therefore solid on overall throughput, though still left more than half the listed docket unaddressed.

Judge-wise, **H.P. Sandesh & P. Sree Sudha** led the day with **22 of 29 matters heard (75.9%)**, well ahead of the other boards. **S.R. Krishna Kumar** handled the largest volume, hearing **103 of 238 cases (43.3%)**, which made that bench the single biggest contributor in absolute disposals despite a mid-range conversion rate. **M.G.S. Kamal** appeared on two listed boards and together heard **89 of 207 matters (43.0%)**, broadly in line with the court-wide average.

Stage conversion was the clearest story. **Preliminary Hearing** moved fastest, with **95 of 114 matters heard (83.3%)**, while **Admission** was moderate at **57.6%**. By contrast, **Orders** was the main drag: only **38 of 169 matters (22.5%)** were heard. **Final Hearing** was also thin at **2 of 8 (25.0%)**. Compared with the historical snapshots, today’s **Orders** conversion was weaker than **24.2%** on 2026-04-24, **36.8%** on 2026-04-17, and **48.8%** on 2026-04-30, suggesting the day favored early-stage movement over end-stage clearance.

By case type, the docket was dominated by **WP (235 scheduled)** and **CRL.P (164)**, together making up about **84% of all listings**. **WP** delivered **106 hearings (45.1%)**, better than the historical WP rates in the retrieved context (**36.4%, 31.7%, and 36.1%**). **CRL.P** converted at **40.9%**, while smaller categories such as **WA (83.3%)**, **RFA (66.7%)**, and **CRL.A (62.5%)** performed better but on much lighter volume. Overall, clearance strength came mainly from high preliminary-hearing movement within the large WP and criminal petition streams, not from strong disposal of orders or final-hearing matters.
